events.description:
I feel like I am the only person I know with IIH. It's coming up to almost two years now and everything still feels like a slog; some days are like wading through mud. I totally get the side effects of medication - everything was stable and then for whatever reason in April it wasn't and we had to re-adjust everything.

I guess I just wanted to be able to communicate with people who understands what it feels like, the tiredness and having to drag yourself through life!

It's going to sound weird, but just reading all your comments above has helped. Not that I am pleased or anything about what you are going through, more that I am reading and going "That's me too - I am not making it up"
Being able to take a break when I need to.

It is great that you are able to prioritise your self-care, taking breaks when you need them. I like to have a stretch and a drink when I take a break, though sometimes it's simply resting. How do you like to take a break?

I worked and studied full-time just before my diagnosis too. I had just got my PhD and was all engaged with the academic world, then all of a sudden I was having to pace myself in a way I didn't before. People treated me differently and I just couldn't deal with it. I am still trying to develop strategies to help me study/research.
Go for a short walk outside on Saturday morning . I will aim to do this Once a week to start with.

This is a great goal and I like that you are planning it once a week to start with. I note that your confidence is low at the moment are there any other ideas or strategies that could help you with this? Can you think of a treat that would help or a nice location for your walk? However it is also important not to pressure yourself and if finding a location is too much starting with nearby for example jut around to the block or walking for a set amount of time or steps may help? Amanda ☺
For the online cafe and being able to talk to people who didn't think I was crazy.
Emma thank you for this lovely feedback. It was such a lovely cafe yesterday sharing, chatting and lots of laughter. You are most definitely not crazy! Hope to see you again. Amanda ☺
Hi Emma, How did you get on with your goal? Please do update but also remember that it is ok to review and adjust your goal. Life and IIH can make you need to change plans? Amanda

I found this online and have been using it to explain to a whole load of people what I have found difficult to communicate before. They have said it is really useful because they didn't get it at all before and now they can at least understand a bit

I am going to set up a note book to help me keep a track of the research projects I have, but break them down into smaller bits. on I am going to set it up during this week.. I will aim to do this Hopefully as a kind of ongoing system?

I am so pleased you found this, and it has been useful. When I first read it, I felt like I was not alone anymore. It is a great idea to share it with others to help explain how you are, though I have shared it on my Facebook, you have inspired me to print it to show it to people in person too. 🙂

I'm intrigued. What are the research projects that you mentioned?  Please don't feel obliged to answer. I hope you find keeping a notebook helpful. Making it into a journal sounds like a great idea too. I shall look forward to finding out how you have got on next week 🙂

To be honest, your idea has inspired me, and I might incorporate it with my current studies too. 🙂

I started a new job yesterday. It's "only" a few hours on two days of the week but it's out, teaching students and is going to be something different on my CV for when I can eventually build back up to closer to full time again.
@emma223 massive congratulations! Manageable steps are important. Good luck.
My daughter, who does not like hugs, just came in and have me a hug.
The information on painkillers was interesting, as I had just been told to avoid them altogether by the neurologist.
I "finished" my goal, or rather started a continuous one... This is the only place I could think of to put a picture of my research notebook
Love this gratitude Amanda ☺

Wow there is a lot of work in here! Its great to have long term and short term goals. You can use the short term goals to work towards something in the long term. Make sure the goals are achievable though and set time frames for the individual steps. In this way you can have the satisfaction of achieving the goals along the way. Amanda ☺
Trying to get to sleep is tough. Properly waking up can take ages if my head is "wobbly." I would love to be able to have a set schedule but I just can't.

My dad used this story in his speech at my wedding but I sort of forgot. I get the mum guilt thing. I am trying to learn to pace myself and let them learn to do stuff too. Reforming my control freak tendencies is taking time mind you!
I am going to pre-read for work this week. on I did some today and will finish tomorrow. . I will aim to do this Aiming for Fri and Sat pm
Emma this is great planning ahead. Your confidence is a bit low at 6 is there any thing else you could add to increase this? Amanda ☺
How are you getting on with your pre reading goal Emma?  Amanda
I have made it through the first week of my new job. Shattered, but no major "migraine" episode.
Emma Well done and enjoy the rest. Hope you are enjoying your new role. Amanda ☺

My worst setback was in April. I had been weened off of all my medication, was feeling ok, but went to the opthalmist (probably wrong term for "eye person") who found swelling on my optic nerve and put me back on acetozolomide straight away. I was really grey for a while, but I have kind of got used to all the medication again. It might sound weird but I make sure I have multicoloured containers to sort my pills in, or a cheerful water cup. I find coloured, cheerful or fun decorations on things make them less oppressive for me.

My opthalmist is brilliant, my neurologist not so much. I have only seen him once (fifteen months ago) despite him supposed to be monitoring  me. I have managed to push for an appointment: they had no capacity, but found one in December. I am actually dreading it because he didn't listen the last time and wasn't actually trying to understand.

I was actually made redundant about the time I was diagnosed. I knew the redundancy date was coming - it had been set before everything with the IIH kicked off. So I had a month or so, then I worked a flexible research contract, where I worked at home or in the office, adapting place and time to how I felt. I was great, but unfortunately was a shorter foxed-term contract. I did a part-time teaching job, which taught me there was no way I could go back to schools work full time. I am currently working as an hourly paid lecturer at a university. Not ideal for security, but I have set hours and they offer me more which I can accept or decline. So far it is going ok, despite the tiredness, because I can go in for 10am and leave when the session is finished.
Churros. I was catching up on some admin that I should have done yesterday, except I was in bed. My husband made me churros and chocolate sauce 😁
Oh my days does he do delivery! That sounds delicious and how lovely of your husband. Amanda ☺

It's more about just being cheerful. So I have a pink computer mouse, a purple keyboard that "clicks" like a typewriter (I always wanted a typewriter). I just like bright but not garish, if that makes sense?

An odd gratitude, but I had a pretty sucky situation at work. Ironically it was my IIH that made me go, "Nope, not taking this - my needs are just as important." I sent it up the chain before I over thought, have a meeting tomorrow, then ate a doughnut (incidentally, yes Aldi's are not as good as Lidl 🤣). So I am grateful that I didn't break into pieces and stood up for myself.

@emma223 well done for sticking up for yourself. It is such a brave but important thing to do. Something, if I'm honest, I am not great at. I hope your meeting goes well tomorrow. It's useful to know about the doughnuts, thank you 🙂
Stickers. I know I am a "grown-up" but I like stickers to cheer up work- related stuff 😁
Love these! Amanda ☺
I want to publish my PhD as an actual book. IIH kind of stalled that process, but I have tried to start in up again.
I used to do creative bits and pieces. For some reason I haven't found a new "craft" to use for zoning out. So I guess my target should be finding one!

I have starting parking a little bit further away when I go to work, meaning I have to work that little bit further each day too.  on Each time I get called in. . I will aim to do this It has been four days a week at the minute.
Emma I really hope that you are able to do this. Please take your time I know publishing and writing for publication can be quite time consuming. Keeping fingers crossed for you. Amanda ☺
Emma this is a great goal and and you are clearly thinking about ways to make small changes that fit in with your daily life. Enjoy the latte! Amanda ☺

events.description: I've joined the programme having been recently diagnosed as I've been struggling to come to terms with living with IHH and the changes in my life. I've also not had much help from medical professionals aside from being told to lose weight (which so far hasn't helped) so I've been feeling a little lost. Hoping that the programme can give me a a bit of connection and help me feel less isolated.
I'm grateful to be able to rest this morning with a cup of tea. It eases the pressure I've been putting on myself just to enjoy the slow moments.
Bethanie treasuring the slow moments is so important. A cup of tea is one of my favourite treats Amanda ☺
Start a new book this week, even just a page. on Thursday after work on the sofa. I will aim to do this Once a week to begin with

A great goal. What type of books do you like to read? Have you a title already picked out? 

I'm looking forward to finding out how you got on next week 🙂

Ooh I love a Richard Osman, didn't realise he had a new one out that may be my treat for reading this week. I can see that you have found reading at the end of the day at the moment doesn't work but that you will be reading at lunchtime instead. Reviewing goals like this is really positive. Let us know how you get on Amanda
I'm going to make time sit and watch married at first sight when they air. on Each evening it's on.. I will aim to do this Monday to Thursday night
I confess to never having watched this but have friends who love it! I hope you enjoy it and I may have to try an episode too! Looking forward to hearing how you get on. Amanda ☺
I found this tricky, think it's going to be one I'll have to practice. I can definitely see how it can be useful!

How did you get on with your watching? Remember it is absolutely ok to reshedule your goal as IIH and life often get in the way. I had to reset my goal from last week due to poorly child and labrador. Amanda
Great feedback and glad you got to enjoy some unwind time!
I think health in general has become much more of a priority for me since my diagnosis. I feel like I need to do the little things now to look after myself a bit more.
I'm going to go into work one day this week on Anyday this week that I'm feeling good. I will aim to do this Just once this week

I've had my first set back this past two weeks since being diagnosed in May. I caught a chest infection (I have asthma as well) and then ended up in A&E with postural hypotension. They weren't sure if it was related to the IIH. I'm recovering now, but I didn't realise how much harder it would be to get over being ill with the IIH and the side effects from the medication as well.

I am just returning to the office after my diagnosis and currently waiting for an occupational health appointment to discuss going from 3 days in the office to 2 days. I find wfh much more manageable as my commute is quite far.
I want to do my masters degree

Bethanie this is a great goal but please don't put lots of pressure on yourself with a time limit. As you say seeing your colleagues face to face is important to you and I hope that this will be a positive experience. I love the reward what a great incentive. Amanda ☺

Wow a masters degree this is a great hope There are also lots more flexible ways to do post graduate study these days and more flexible timeframes than in the past. It is good that they recognise more that this is important but please do consider your needs alongside your choice of course and good luck. Amanda ☺
I'm grateful for my slow cooker! It's the first time I was able to prepare a proper meal for a few weeks and I wouldn't have been able to do it without my slow cooker!

@bethanie8 I am really pleased you managed to have a good meal using your slow cooker. I find ours so useful, though we haven't had a meal from ours for a while. You have inspired me to will get it out this week. 🙂
I'm going to say no to additional work this week on Wednesday at work. I will aim to do this This week, but will practice saying no more

I love fibre arts especially embroidery and cross stitch (I am a terrible knitter!). I especially like projects that relate to the female experience since for a long time the fibre arts were looked down upon as 'not actual art' and we've lost so much traditional fibre knowledge. 
I haven't managed to pick my projects back up since my diagnosis, but I think I might be ready to. 
I love the fact that some so small as a needle and thread can create beautiful pieces and that you don't need much to get started. I'll post a picture of my current work and I'm feeling a bit inspired to pick it up again.

@ruth40 I've never tried throwing pottery but I love watching the great pottery throw down! I do enjoy pottery painting though, I like trying to figure out what colours the paints are going to come out as 😊

My neurologist has been good so far, I've only seen him twice though, once when I was diagnosed and one follow up. He has had to re-refer me to the eye clinic as I've fallen off their list and not had any follow up with them at all, so it's hard to say what state my eyes are in.
The thing I have found though is that professionals want to look at things separately which is frustrating when things could be linked. I was experiencing postural hypotension last week (it's better now), but no one seemed to know or particularly care about whether it could have been linked to the IIH and they refused to contact my neurologist/neurology. It's difficult to advocate for yourself when you're in A&E at 1am and you're very unwell!

@ruth40 I haven't even ventured into crochet, but my friend has promised she will teach me so I will give it a go! I definitely will do, I'm feeling more inspired about it than I have in a long time 😊

I like this goal and totally get the challenge of not putting off your laptop and additional work. It is really important as you say not to pressure yourself and to also look after your work life balance especially with IIH. I look forward to seeing your halloween pens! Amanda☺

As promised, here is a picture of my current embroidery. I'm grateful that I'm still able to sew and for feeling more inspired lately! (Not my design and I'm sorry for the bad language on my needle minder 😂)
Bethanie this is a fabulous design no apologies needed for the language! Thank you for sharing your work ☺
Bethanie how did you get on with your goal? I hope that you have managed to get the pens! Amanda
@bethanie8 this is so lovely and well done on achieving your goal. It is great to hear that you are planning to go in to see colleagues again next week. The craft fair sounds lovely! Amanda

@bethanie8 well done on keeping your boundaries.

events.description:
IIH has led to big changes in my life and how I view myself.
I’m hoping the Hope course will help me find the best route through this.
I’m grateful for the companionship of my dog Buddy. He keeps me motivated to get some exercise each day. ❤️
@ruth39 Buddy is gorgeous. Our furry family members really do make days brighter. It's great that walking Buddy helps you to get some exercise.
Create some photosensitive artwork  on In the kitchen and garden. Over the next week.. I will aim to do this Once
@ruth40 I am intrigued. What is photosensitive artwork? I look forward to finding out how you got on. If you feel confident enough, it would be great if you could share something you created. 🙂

Sleep is an ongoing battle, I wake through out the night and have to relax to drop off. For me the best way to relax my body and mind is listening to stories. I use an eye mask with built in headphones and this seems to work most of the time for me, but still feel I often live in sleep debt that I struggle to catch up on.
Wow these are amazing! Sorry to hear the paint is not the one you wanted but I think they look fabulous and I love the idea of this. Amanda

For me IIH has changed my life significantly. Work was always something I enjoyed and was important to me. Unfortunately IIH made it impossible for me to continue to work. I am now medically retired and the future looks very different for me. This video is a reminder of what the most important parts of my life are and to focus on those blessings.
To meet up with my friend for a catch up. on Next week, in a cafe or at one of our houses.. I will aim to do this Monthly
Ruth this is a lovely goal and I look forward to hearing how it goes. Time with friends is so important and enjoy the cake! Amanda ☺
I’m grateful to my daughter and husband who drive me to my pottery course. Without them I would of had to stop after suddenly having to stop driving.
To finish planting my spring bulbs  on Next Tuesday in the front and back garden.. I will aim to do this Hopefully it will take just one day to finish

@ruth40 such a lovely gratitude. I am really pleased you are still able to go to your pottery class. Is it something you have been doing for a while? I would love to see one of your makes, if you are happy to share 🙂
@ruth40 such a lovely goal, to plant and grow spring flowers, but with the added bonus of knowing they can be used at your daughter's wedding. Congratulations to your daughter and partner.

Being creative is what keeps me going. My biggest passion is pottery. I enjoy the therapeutic benefits of the clay in my hands and how immersed I become in the creation. I also dabble in other creative media too.
I will finish glazing my fairy houses and fairy doors and fire them. on At the kitchen table. . I will aim to do this 3 days a week
@ruth40 those fairy houses look amazing! I shall look forward to seeing them completed. 🙂I have a major weak spot for fairies and related fairy things.
@ruth40, well done on completing your goal. I really hope they do well for you, and can be used at your daughter's wedding
I too am a terrible knitter, I’m also awful at crochet but enjoy the experience. You should give your projects a try, you may find really helps you.

@Kerry_Chalmers-Worth_ thank you. I love the great pottery throw down, it was what made me want to do pottery over lock down. So I taught myself some basic skills. I have since down a couple of short courses which I’ve enjoyed.
@bethanie8 I do both throwing and hand building. I started hand building with air dry clay. I just love the feel of clay in my hands , turning from a lump into something.
I’m glad you are feeling inspired. I definitely find the benefits to my mental health out weigh how hard it can be to find the motivation to do things.😊

@ruth40 did you manage to glaze and fire the fairy houses and doors? If not, don't worry, life happens, with Hope we like to take a compassionate approach, be kind to yourself. You can update your goal if you need more time, simply by pressing the 3 little dots in the top right corner.

events.description:
I have joined the programme for many of the reasons others have already said. 
I feel like my life has been turned upside down, and am finding living with IIH very overwhelming and difficult. Was diagnosed almost a year ago, but had symptoms for over a year before that which was 1st treated as a vestibular disorder, which just made me feel worse. And have had very little support or advice about IIH from health professionals since being diagnosed. I used to work full time and study on the side and that's all stopped, people I thought were friends have disappeared since I no longer work and socialise with them and my anxiety is the worst it's ever been. And as much as my family try to support and help me it's hard for them to understand when they know very little about the condition.

Guess I'm hoping the course will help me feel less negative and also connect with people who understand.

@emma223 
I'm sorry to hear you're experiencing this too, it's so frustrating to not be able to do the things you did before or things you want to do, isn't it! As awful as it sounds it is comforting to know there are others experiencing the same issues and understand what it's like.
I'm grateful for feeling well enough to visit my aunt and uncle yesterday and meeting their adorable new puppy Mac!!
Oh my gosh he is adorable and very happy you got to meet him! I am loving seeing everyone's dog photos (all other pet photos welcomed!). Amanda ☺

To complete some of the Hope programme every other day - I will aim to do this for at least 30 minutes each time. on I will aim for every other day.. I will aim to do this Will do this for 30 minutes every other day.

This is a lovely goal, I will look forward to finding out how you got on next week 🙂. I notice your confidence level is a 5. Is there some way you could alter this goal to make it feel more achievable? Perhaps doing the course in smaller chunks or on fewer days?
Donna how are you getting on with your goal of working on the HOPE programme? Amanda

Donna please don't be frustrated things do get in the way and amending or changing goals is absolutely fine. I am sorry that birthday celebrations increased your symptoms and hope this week will be more settled for you. Amanda

Bit late putting it on here but I'm grateful for the support of a good friend! 
At the weekend a friend of mine travelled 2 hours from where I used to live to see me! She was very supportive and understanding, and was happy to do whatever I could manage as my symptoms weren't great over the weekend. Was lovely to see her as it had been over 2 years since we last saw each other.
@donna62 a gratitude like this is never too late! I am pleased your friend is being so supportive. I hope you manage to meet up again sooner than 2 years. 🙂
To go for a walk  on Local park - will aim for Tuesdays and Thursdays. . I will aim to do this I will aim for twice a week
I want to finish the Advanced Diploma I was doing before my IIH symptoms started!

events.description: Hi Sheila this is a lovely gratitude enjoy your holiday!😊
Such a gorgeous picture. Sight is so precious, a really good thing to be grateful for 😊

After being diagnosed with IIH  in April and then having a heart attack in June and losing my mother in law Aug
I enjoy each day as if it could be my last.
A fabulous picture and great to hear that he puts a smile on your face. My labradors do the same for me Amanda 😊
Oh wow Sheila fabulous picture and yes I can imagine feeling the wind in your face will be wonderful. It is great weather at the moment long may that continue! 😊
I get very tired, which affects my eye sight. When I get like this I go have a lie down and think of all the things I have to look forward too.

events.description: Hi I’m Jo, I joined because I need to gain some confidence back. I was diagnosed 9 months ago and lost so much but am now starting to take control back and rebuilding my new life. IIH was such a shock and I had so little information on what it was, I got quite good at researching and found support groups online and shared experiences with others and found this really helped. It was the depression that followed diagnosis that made me realise I needed support from others like this Hope Course. Thank you to all of you for providing it to us and I’m looking forward to sharing and learning.

@joanna40  a lovely goal to nurture and grow from seed.  Remembering why it's important to you might just give your confidence an extra boost.   And, I wonder if your photo to your daughter might encourage her to do the same with you?  P.S. Lovely photo of your garden.

On these days and sadly they are often I will go back to my bedroom with my dogs and a hot water bottle. Curtains closed and lights off. I use my pillows to hold me up as laying down is painful and I just shut my eyes and drift as much as I can. Always drink lots of water and try not to panic as this always makes my headaches worse.

I blame mine on the medication but often it’s my low mood. If I have a busy day or see my grown up kids, the next day I find I’m exhausted. I call them my poorly days. PJs on and hide from the world just have to be careful I don’t slip into to many days like these.

@joanna40 I really love this goal. I'm looking forward to finding out how you get on 😊 I notice your confidence of completing this goal is only 4. events.description: I feel that my life has been ripped from underneath me and I just don't recognise myself since my diagnosis. A lot of professionals or family I speak to think that all I need to do is lose weight and I will be better and don't seem to understand quite how much this is impacting me. I am hoping I will gain a lot from hearing other people's stories and find some ways to cope and maybe even enjoy life again
I am grateful for my partner, who is extremely supportive and understanding, especially on my worst days
@hannah69 it is great that your partner is supportive and that you recognise this with gratitute, I must admit I often take my husband for granted lol

Have a think about making the goal achievable for you sometimes doing something every day can be hard you might want to start at a couple of times a week for example? It might also be useful to use a timer or set yourself a number of chapters or pages so that you can feel you are achieving? Sometimes even picking a book to read is a goal for me! Amanda ☺

I can't comment on effective methods, as I am still trying to find a treatment that helps my headaches, but I gained a lot from the video and hoping to be able to take some of that information to my next appointment to potentially explore migraine treatments

It's hard to identify a sole cause of my fatigue. I know my mental health isn't great, a lot of medical professionals say this could be the cause of my fatigue, but then the fatigue is one of the reasons I am struggling to improve my mental health, so I feel I am in a never ending cycle. Hopefully one day I can find ways that can help me to get out of it!

My diagnosis of IIH is relatively new (April 2025), I feel like I am still at the stage where I am struggling to come to terms with how my life plan has had to change. This video is really thought provoking, because even if I can't go to work currently or do the things I used to do, I have rocks in my life who I should focus on and appreciate more.

events.description: Hi Everyone, sorry I'm late joining, past couple of weeks have been somewhat hectic! I was diagnosed with IIH in 2010 and IIH has been lifechanging. adjusting to change has been difficult and It helps knowing i'm not alone in this experience! I find the cognitive / memory problems particularly difficult especially as this seems to be getting worse.
Managing stress in one of my greatest challenges. I particularly enjoyed the streams exercise and will research more about mindfulness.
Thank you, will give it a go!
Sit and read a book for enjoyment on An hour every afternoon at home (not vicarage). I will aim to do this Start once a week building up to every day
I have been prescribed melatonin which helps greatly as im not producing it naturally. I sleep all night but wake up feeling exhausted.
Work was a big part of my life, but had to accept medical retirement. I now have a new job, and trying hard not yo let it take over my life, and make more time for family and enjoying life

events.description: I have joined the HOPE programme because, like others have mentioned, dealing with IIH can feel overwhelming and isolating. I attended the IIH conference in June and found it extremely helpful, especially the talk by one of the neurologists about research on migraines and IIH. The research he shared literally changed my life, as it informed a medication approach that has made a huge difference for me. It was also wonderful to interact with others who truly understand what this condition feels like. Through this programme, I hope to gain a sense of community and connection, and to engage with others who share similar experiences.

I'm grateful that my dad helped move the sofa so I could hoover behind. I enjoy making sure everywhere is super clean. I also went to the hospital this morning for an unrelated IIH issue and the specialist was really nice, understanding and comforting so that made me feel really good.
I am going to go for a walk or swim 4 times a week  on Swimming at the gym or walking at a park. I will aim to do this 4 times a week
I really like this activity, I have used it before and it has been so helpful during anxious times.
I'm going to start a new series  on At home on the sofa. I will aim to do this Evenings whenever I have time

When I get migraines I like to use a cold pack that goes round my head, I drink electrolytes, lay down in a dark room, and try and sleep. Sometimes I use a silicone head scrubber that is normally used when people have showers to distribute product, but it improves blood circulation and isn't too rough so it feels like a scalp massage. It also has the benefit of promoting healthier hair! I used to rely quite heavily on OTC painkillers as candesartan was not working for me as first given by my neurologist. I am now on atogepants which I found information about from one of the talks at the conference, I feel like these are working quite, I've only been on a month but have found that the debilitating migraines have decreased to 2 times a month instead of 2 times a week. I'm excited to see how I will be when I've been on the drug for 3 months. I think the Headache diary downloadable is great and I am going to use that. Thank you

I find it hard to sleep due to tinnitus but I am currently under tinnitus management service and I have been using a recording they sent me to help. I also used to have 2 pillows but I felt like they weren't firm enough and I didn't feel elevated enough and I had struggles with dizziness when I was getting up, but I have started using my knee pillow and my face fits into the gap perfectly!! I've found that is a lot firmer and while not as comfy, I am getting a better night sleep than I was.

replies.text: Hi all, my name is Bethan and I am one of your facilitators for the next 6 weeks.  I'm really looking forward to getting to know you and hear more about why you've joined the programme.  I hope you enjoy taking your first steps 💛.

Hi everyone, I'm Kerry another of your course facilitators. I have IIH myself and as a participant previously found the Hope course very helpful. I'm looking forward to finding out a bit more about you and supporting you though the course journey. I really hope you enjoy the first week of the programme 😊

I joined the course because I want to actively feel like I'm in control. I want to get the things in my life back that feel I've lost. I need guidance and support from others who understand the obstacles we face. My employer treats me like I'm ill but I'm not I have a disability not an illness that's how I see IIH. I'm here to learn how to support myself.

Hi, I'm Amanda and I am one of your facilitators. I live with IIH and have been involved in the setting up of the HOPE course and as a participant. I am looking forward to getting to know you all over the next 6 weeks and supporting you on your HOPE journey. 😊

@lizzie10 Hi Lizzie, Thank you for sharing why you are taking part in the HOPE programme. If you are able to join the HOPE cafe (there are 3 for this course) they are a really good opportunity to talk to other people with IIH and also to the facilitators of the course. We will be looking at gratitude and goal setting which can be really positive tools. Amanda 😊

Hi I’m Jo, I joined because I need to gain some confidence back. I was diagnosed 9 months ago and lost so much but am now starting to take control back and rebuilding my new life. IIH was such a shock and I had so little information on what it was, I got quite good at researching and found support groups online and shared experiences with others and found this really helped. It was the depression that followed diagnosis that made me realise I needed support from others like this Hope Course. Thank you to all of you for providing it to us and I’m looking forward to sharing and learning.

Hi Im Tracey im 47,  I was diagnosed in December last year, for me its triggered some real health anxieties, I had a hysterectomy at 24 as an emergency and a real rare case, my dad has really rare illness he's the only one in the uk , I saved his life when he had a cardiac arrest a couple of years ago and I think having the urgency of your not leaving the hospital following seeing the ophthalmologist, its really triggered something. I feel really annoyed that I feel so much worst since starting medication. I think the struggle is everyone saying it's just a headache. I can feel myself avoiding things for fear of the what ifs and ive been there before and really don't want to be back in the place so im  looking forward to the Hope course and hope that it can be a positive step in taking control.

@joanna39 A warm welcome Joanna. the Hope course is a great place to discover strategies that can help you self-manage, some will resonate with you more than others, which is good as you can tailor your Hope experience to fit with you. It's also a great place to meet others and share experiences, in the forum and during our informal, relaxed cafes.

Hiya Everyone, im Natalie, I joined the hope programme as after being diagnosed over 23 years ago, there still isnt enough information regarding this condition and even now 23 years of having to deal with situations, can still be very overwhelming, just hoping to be able to offer some support and also learn new things. Thank you to everyone.

@tracey69 Hi Tracey, I am sorry to hear that you have had such a tough time with your own health and your dads health. It is really frustrating when people dismiss the IIH headache and don't understand what it is like. If you are able to join the HOPE cafe these are a great opportunity to meet and talk to other people with IIH who get it. Amanda

Hello everyone I’m Rosemary. I’m 30 years old and was diagnosed with IIH in 2023. I had never heard of IIH before so have done some research and joined support group to try and help me understand but I still don’t really understand if the way I feel are symptoms of IIH so it would be nice to have a better understanding and speak to people with the same condition to help me understand it a bit better.

Hi, I’m Bryony. I’m 36 and have had IIH since 2011. I’ve tried all sorts of treatments over the years and have a shunt. I joined the course as I always feel lonely and low. I’m hoping to learn how to live again and be around more people that understand this condition.

Hi, I’m Brie and I’m 32. I have just been officially diagnosed with IIH in February. I had been experiencing symptoms for almost three years but had given up on finding the root cause of them until an opticians visit for new contact lenses flagged it. I joined this course to be able to learn tools for managing living with IIH and also connect with those who have been on this journey that can relate to the challenges I’ve faced.

Hi, my name is Amanda, I am 40 yrs old. I have been diagnosed with IIH for 12 years now. I have an LP shunt which has been fine (mostly) until Nov 18th last year, when it malfunctioned. Long story short I ended up having 3 surgeries in 3 months and I am now writing this from a hospital bed as I'm back  in again since Friday with pressure issues again! I'm waiting to see what they plan to do! I joined the course to learn some techniques to cope and also meet people in a similar position.

@natalie62 Hi Natalie, welcome in, it's great to have you here. Here's hoping that the information shared in the weekly sessions will provide you with more information to support you to manage situations ahead.  Enjoy taking part and the Forum is a great place to reach out to each other.

@rosemary16, welcome to Hope, you've come to the right  place.  Thank you for sharing your story and for joining the conversation.  We look at IIH symptoms in more detail in Session 3 and we share real life case stories throughout which might support your understanding.   Enjoy taking part in the activities and reaching out to others.

@bryony7, thank you for sharing your experience, thoughts and feelings.   It sounds like the course has come at the right time for you.  Hope is a supportive space where you can connect with others and pick up useful tools to keep moving forward.  Gratitude is always a good place to start.

@brie1, welcome to your very own online community of Hope 🍵 .  You are not alone. Hope is a great place to connect with others, learn together and share lots of valuable tools for coping.  I'm looking forward to seeing what you take away each week and you can always tell us more in the end of session feedback.

@amanda85, welcome in.  I'm sorry to hear you've had a tough time of it recently but it's great to have you here, albeit from your hospital bed.  Here's hoping you'll be back home soon with a plan and that the techniques shared each week will provide some support. It also sounds like you will get a lot out of being on the programme with others and you can always reach out to us, your facilitators via messages.  Take care.

They weren't sure if it was related to the IIH. I'm recovering now, but I didn't realise how much harder it would be to get over being ill with the IIH and the side effects from the medication as well.

I’ve been struggling since my diagnosis also suffering with other illnesses which seem to be worse since I got this diagnosis the past few leeks have been very hard even to look at my phone to come on here is hard sometimes with my eyes and my head but when I’m on here I do feel very reassured that I’m not alone and find othered are going through the same thing as me I know it’s hard for our partners children etc as some symptoms you can’t see but I find being on here is very helpfull to me

I’ve been struggling since my diagnosis also suffering with other illnesses which seem to be worse since I got this diagnosis the past few leeks have been very hard even to look at my phone to come on here is hard sometimes with my eyes and my head but when I’m on here I do feel very reassured that I’m not alone and find othered are going through the same thing as me I know it’s hard for our partners children etc as some symptoms you can’t see but I find being on here is very helpfull to me
@bethanie8 Sorry to hear about this setback and this is so valid. A lot of people do say that it takes longer to recover from infections when you have IIH too. I hope you are feeling better. Amanda

@jody3 Jody you are most definitely not alone and it is good to hear that the HOPE course is helping with that. Remember that you can do the HOPE course more than once. I am sorry to hear that your other conditions are impacting on your overall health. With the HOPE course try not to do too much in one go little and often may help. You may also find it useful to only do the bits that you can at this time and prioritise areas that may help you at this time. Do reach out if you need some support. Amanda
Thank you I really appreciate that
Thank you I really appreciate that
Thank you I really appreciate that

I admit I have had very mixed experiences with health professionals, some I came away and hid in a corner crying, feeling I hadn't been heard, others where they were extremely helpful and really listened to me. Meeting new health professionals usually causes me anxiety, but I have learnt to use some of the techniques above to help alleviate my stress.

My opthalmist is brilliant, my neurologist not so much. I have only seen him once (fifteen months ago) despite him supposed to be monitoring  me. I have managed to push for an appointment: they had no capacity, but found one in December. I am actually dreading it because he didn't listen the last time and wasn't actually trying to understand.

@Kerry_Chalmers-Worth_  Kerry its great that you find some of the techniques helpful any in particular? For me I need to write questions down on my phone...if I write them on paper I tend to forget the paper.  Otherwise I come out frustrated because I have forgotton something. Amanda ☺
@emma223 Sorry to hear that your neurologist is challenging. It is great that you are advocating for your self and getting appointments. Amanda ☺

My neurologist has been good so far, I've only seen him twice though, once when I was diagnosed and one follow up. He has had to re-refer me to the eye clinic as I've fallen off their list and not had any follow up with them at all, so it's hard to say what state my eyes are in.
The thing I have found though is that professionals want to look at things separately which is frustrating when things could be linked. I was experiencing postural hypotension last week (it's better now), but no one seemed to know or particularly care about whether it could have been linked to the IIH and they refused to contact my neurologist/neurology. It's difficult to advocate for yourself when you're in A&E at 1am and you're very unwell!

@bethanie8 It is great to hear about your positive experiences with your neurologist there are some really good ones out there. I am sorry that the lack of joined up services is frustrating. It is as you say hard to advocate for yourself at 1am but well done you for doing so. I hope you are feeling better. Amanda ☺
I do a lot of sewing and sewing greetings cards with the help of my magnifying glass.
I knit, clay work and paint. I’m not great at it but I do enjoy making beautiful things. My knitting has been my friend since I was 10 and always my go to during tough times.
@sheila15  Sheila these are great creative activities. A magnifying glass is a great tip. Amanda 😊

@joanna40  Joanna these are great creative hobbies. Having a range of things to do can be really helpful so that you can pick what works for you at that time. I am very envious of your knitting ability I have tried several times and it is beyond my co-ordination skills! Amanda 😊

I have started back into jigsaw puzzles and am getting a favourite one framed. I am also an avid reader and have set a realistic goal to read 24 books this year, but also journaling what I liked about the book beside a printed picture of the cover.

@sinead4 Sinead This is great. My son and I just completed a jigsaw and we really struggled to put it away after...we should have framed it! Reading is fabulous and what a great plan regarding journaling about the books. 😊

Being creative is what keeps me going. My biggest passion is pottery. I enjoy the therapeutic benefits of the clay in my hands and how immersed I become in the creation. I also dabble in other creative media too.

@ruth40 the fairy houses you are aiming to complete this week look amazing. You have a real talent for pottery. 🙂 Like you I like to use my hands but simply for colouring, I find similar benefits to you doing pottery.

I love fibre arts especially embroidery and cross stitch (I am a terrible knitter!). I especially like projects that relate to the female experience since for a long time the fibre arts were looked down upon as 'not actual art' and we've lost so much traditional fibre knowledge. 
I haven't managed to pick my projects back up since my diagnosis, but I think I might be ready to. 
I love the fact that some so small as a needle and thread can create beautiful pieces and that you don't need much to get started. I'll post a picture of my current work and I'm feeling a bit inspired to pick it up again.

@ruth40 I've never tried throwing pottery but I love watching the great pottery throw down! I do enjoy pottery painting though, I like trying to figure out what colours the paints are going to come out as 😊
I too am a terrible knitter, I’m also awful at crochet but enjoy the experience. You should give your projects a try, you may find really helps you.

@Kerry_Chalmers-Worth_ thank you. I love the great pottery throw down, it was what made me want to do pottery over lock down. So I taught myself some basic skills. I have since down a couple of short courses which I’ve enjoyed.
@bethanie8 I do both throwing and hand building. I started hand building with air dry clay. I just love the feel of clay in my hands , turning from a lump into something.

@ruth40 I haven't even ventured into crochet, but my friend has promised she will teach me so I will give it a go! I definitely will do, I'm feeling more inspired about it than I have in a long time 😊
I’m glad you are feeling inspired. I definitely find the benefits to my mental health out weigh how hard it can be to find the motivation to do things.😊

@bethanie8 Fibre arts what a lovely term I hadn't heard of it before. I really hope that you are able to pick up your projects again soon as crafting can have lots of benefits for you. Have a think about how you could build this in even in very small time periods. It would be lovely to see a picture of your work. Amanda ☺
@ruth40 Totally agree with this sentiment. Sometimes setting goals in these areas can be really helpful and remembering to prioritise things that support your mental health. Amanda ☺
I used to do creative bits and pieces. For some reason I haven't found a new "craft" to use for zoning out. So I guess my target should be finding one!

I have stopped being creative. I need to re engage with painting and writing. I have ADHD as well as IIH and have been struggling a lot with focus and concentration. I have been spending a lot of time and effort trying to be more productive but I really feel I have lost touch with myself a bit. I also need time to day dream and doodle  and just be.

I have stopped being creative. I need to re engage with painting and writing. I have ADHD as well as IIH and have been struggling a lot with focus and concentration. I have been spending a lot of time and effort trying to be more productive but I really feel I have lost touch with myself a bit. I also need time to day dream and doodle  and just be.
@emma223 Finding a new craft is a great target. I am very craft challenged but have finally found something within my capabilities...block printing. I look forward to hearing what you find! Amanda ☺

@helen41 Please don't be hard on yourself. I have ADHD as well as IIH and focusing is hard. Have a think about how you can find time to day dream and doodle such as setting aside a small amount of time when you can, setting alarms, setting small steps towards a goal for example. I also find telling my partner helps so that he makes me step away from the mundane everyday tasks like ironing etc to take time for me. Prioritise you time. Amanda ☺
I did the test and I got Appreciation of Beauty and Excellence. And I guess I do see things others don’t sometimes. In the world around us and in people
@joanna40, that is so very lovely. It is so nice to be made aware of some of your strengths, as we don't often appreciate ourselves enough. I have yet to do mine.
Let me know what yours says. It’s exciting stuff
@joanna40 I will I'm going on the course this evening.

My top three  on the test were kindness, love and fairness. 

Surprisingly Hope came 10th. I expected social intelligence to come higher than 7 as I usually score highly on empathy, but I guess these are slightly different things. Plus I can be really quiet in a crowd 🤣

I blame mine on the medication but often it’s my low mood. If I have a busy day or see my grown up kids, the next day I find I’m exhausted. I call them my poorly days. PJs on and hide from the world just have to be careful I don’t slip into to many days like these.

@joanna40 You are so right that fatigue can have so many reasons. It can be really hard with IIH to know how much is too much if that makes sense. I hope the HOPE materials help you with strategies for managing fatigue Amanda maaging this.

I have been a bad sleeper for a long time. More recently around the time of the IIH diagnosis’s I was diagnosed with sleep apnoea. A revelation and explained waking up with heart racing etc. Sleep has become less enjoyable with the paraphernalia of masks and tubes but I am grateful for the quality of sleep when I do sleep.
A racing brain keeps me awake. And I have developed a bad habit of listening to things with headphones to drown out the tinnitus because I think I’m realising this isn’t allowing my brain to rest.
I’m going to look at some of the guides on IIH website.

@Mikki Hi Mikki, Thank you for sharing. Racing brains can be a challenge getting to sleep. Session 5 includes a short breathing and mindfulness session. On the IIH UK You Tube Channel is a short body scan which you might find useful. 
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CRqd90dYH70 Once you have done it a couple of times its something that you can do in your head. I hope these are useful but it is also something I am going to start a forum post to see if anyone is able to share their tips for sleep with IIH as it is such an important area. Amanda
Trying to get to sleep is tough. Properly waking up can take ages if my head is "wobbly." I would love to be able to have a set schedule but I just can't.

Emma I totally agree. Are there any things that help you to get to sleep? If anyone else has any strategies that help them please do comment and share. I am quite sensory and really find sleep sprays help me but I know for a lot of people this is a marmite one and can work the other way. I also have a favorite pillow and bed socks. Amanda ☺

@emma223 totally agree sleep can be challenging and it something if affected that can have a massive impact day to day. I like sleep sprays but I am quite sensory so know this has the opposite effect for some. I also have favourite pillows and bed socks. Does anyone else have sleep strategies that they can share? Amanda ☺

I just wouldn’t be with out my long hot water bottle. I have brought a few different pillows but have finally found one that doesn’t feel like I’m laying my head on a brick. The tinnitus at night is still an issue and nothing seems to help that. I have used eye masks on really bad nights and now have one I can heat or cool down. When my sleep is bad I know my mental health is low and it definitely doesn’t help my IIH. But we keep going 💪🙏🏻😊

@joanna52 Joanna totally agree that sleep has such an impact on everything. Long hot water bottle sounds interesting...does it have a fluffy cover...I saw one in the shops the other day and was tempted! I have a favourite pillow but my son has decided its his favourite too so I need a new one! Amanda ☺

Sleep is an ongoing battle, I wake through out the night and have to relax to drop off. For me the best way to relax my body and mind is listening to stories. I use an eye mask with built in headphones and this seems to work most of the time for me, but still feel I often live in sleep debt that I struggle to catch up on.

It's hard to identify a sole cause of my fatigue. I know my mental health isn't great, a lot of medical professionals say this could be the cause of my fatigue, but then the fatigue is one of the reasons I am struggling to improve my mental health, so I feel I am in a never ending cycle. Hopefully one day I can find ways that can help me to get out of it!

@hannah70 Hannah I totally agree that fatigue can affect your mental health and vice versa. There is at last more recognition that IIH affects mental health and it was something that was discussed at the IIH UK conference this year that more research is needed into IIH and mental health and how to manage it. There are lots of strategies on the HOPE course that may help you with your mental health and fatigue and I hope you find some that will help you. Do reach out if you want to discuss further. Amanda ☺

I find it hard to sleep due to tinnitus but I am currently under tinnitus management service and I have been using a recording they sent me to help. I also used to have 2 pillows but I felt like they weren't firm enough and I didn't feel elevated enough and I had struggles with dizziness when I was getting up, but I have started using my knee pillow and my face fits into the gap perfectly!! I've found that is a lot firmer and while not as comfy, I am getting a better night sleep than I was.

@olivia24 I am pleased you have accessed the tinnitus management service. I admit I hadn't heard of one before. I am pleased you brought it to my attention. I am going to find out if it is available across the UK. 

Getting pillow support right to elevate you and still feel comfortable is trial and error, sadly. I hope you eventually find something more comfortable, it's good that you are able to sleep better now though 🙂
I have been prescribed melatonin which helps greatly as im not producing it naturally. I sleep all night but wake up feeling exhausted.

On these days and sadly they are often I will go back to my bedroom with my dogs and a hot water bottle. Curtains closed and lights off. I use my pillows to hold me up as laying down is painful and I just shut my eyes and drift as much as I can. Always drink lots of water and try not to panic as this always makes my headaches worse.
@joanna40 Hi Joanna, These are great tips and strategies for managing headache. There are some other tips here on the IIH UK website leaflets https://www.iih.org.uk/product/11/2/leaflets
Acupressure mat, hot bath and a cold eye mask.
@lizzie11 These are great ideas for helping with the symptoms of IIH.
I have a migraine cap that I pop in the freezer and it can be helpful, if it isn't to hand a damp towel can help.   I also use a microwavable wrap to warm my neck and shoulders.
@Kerry_Chalmers-Worth_  This is great advice. I have got some of the cooling travel towels that I can carry about with me, you just wet them with water. Easily portable when out and about
The information on painkillers was interesting, as I had just been told to avoid them altogether by the neurologist.

We can't comment on the individual advice given to you by your neurologist as they will have an overview of your history etc. What we can do is discuss information that is out there from the research etc so that you are informed for your discussions with your health care professionals. It may be worth discussing with your neurologist the reasons why they are saying no to anything and asking if there are any that you could take, in what dose and in what situations?   Amanda ☺

I can't comment on effective methods, as I am still trying to find a treatment that helps my headaches, but I gained a lot from the video and hoping to be able to take some of that information to my next appointment to potentially explore migraine treatments

Taking information with you to ask about at your clinic appointment is a really good idea. 

Everyone is different and has different medical histories, so we can not make any individual recommendations. There are many new migraine treatments available. I suggest checking out the migraine trust website for more details.

I hope you find a treatment that works for you. 🙂

@hannah70  Its great that you found the video helpful and I totally agree that finding a treatment for headaches can be challenging. I was chatting with Clare the information rep for IIH UK the other day and she discussed having different treatment approaches for her different headaches. Using a headache and or trigger diary can be helpful to work out different headaches, triggers and then take this with you for discussion with your team. Amanda ☺

When I get migraines I like to use a cold pack that goes round my head, I drink electrolytes, lay down in a dark room, and try and sleep. Sometimes I use a silicone head scrubber that is normally used when people have showers to distribute product, but it improves blood circulation and isn't too rough so it feels like a scalp massage. It also has the benefit of promoting healthier hair! I used to rely quite heavily on OTC painkillers as candesartan was not working for me as first given by my neurologist. I am now on atogepants which I found information about from one of the talks at the conference, I feel like these are working quite, I've only been on a month but have found that the debilitating migraines have decreased to 2 times a month instead of 2 times a week. I'm excited to see how I will be when I've been on the drug for 3 months. I think the Headache diary downloadable is great and I am going to use that. Thank you

@olivia24 I am so very pleased that the information you took from the conference has led to a change in medication that has had such a positive effect. 🙂 The headache diary is such a great tool. I hope you find the same when using it.

31931.title: Talking with your employer or educator

replies.text: I was actually made redundant about the time I was diagnosed. I knew the redundancy date was coming - it had been set before everything with the IIH kicked off. So I had a month or so, then I worked a flexible research contract, where I worked at home or in the office, adapting place and time to how I felt. I was great, but unfortunately was a shorter foxed-term contract. I did a part-time teaching job, which taught me there was no way I could go back to schools work full time. I am currently working as an hourly paid lecturer at a university. Not ideal for security, but I have set hours and they offer me more which I can accept or decline. So far it is going ok, despite the tiredness, because I can go in for 10am and leave when the session is finished.

@emma223 Many people find that IIH impacts significantly on their work. It sounds like you have found a way that you can still do your career but with the flexibility that you need. I am sorry that this has resulted in less job security but well done for recognising what works for you and what is manageable with IIH. Amanda

I am just returning to the office after my diagnosis and currently waiting for an occupational health appointment to discuss going from 3 days in the office to 2 days. I find wfh much more manageable as my commute is quite far.

I’m currently off work since my diagnosis still feel to unwell to return as of yet I am a bit scared of what the future holds I do love my job but I think it will be a case of reducing my days when I do return I work in a busy clinic in nhs so just hope they will accommodate me when the time comes for me to return my managers have been very supportive since I have been off so I’m lucky in that respect

@bethanie8 You are absolutely right in that finding ways of working with your symptoms is so important. WFH can be one way but also think about the office environment etc. Occupational Health can help you with this but if anyone else has any tips to help here please do add them in the discusison. I found getting a special blue tint on my glasses helped with glare and changing office to have access to natural light by a window really helped. Amanda

@jody3 Jody it is really positive that your managers have been supportive. It is definitely worth thinking about what you feel would help to support you in the environment in which you work. Flexible working is definitely one area but think about other areas like lighting, glare, noise etc. Amanda
Loved this exercise and found myself very calm by my stream
@joanna40 It is a lovely exercise and I am glad you enjoyed it Amanda 😊

I noticed just how many thoughts keep popping into my mind in a short space of time even when I’m trying to focus on the breathing, and just being in the moment. Its hard to switch off and can see how because of that, without even knowing it, repeat thoughts grow in volume (almost like the word cloud - more means bigger and therefore has more presence in my thoughts). Will try to do this or other mindfulness tasks more to see if I get any better at it. I sometimes think I’m failing at it because I just can’t focus enough to prevent the thoughts from popping in. But think I’m learning that in some ways that is the point, just be aware and let it go, and there is no judgement.

@Mikki Hi Mikki, This is a great description, a word cloud in your head. You are absolutely not failing at it by having words and thoughts pop into you head this is absolutely what happens. Recognising and acknowledging the thoughts as they come in can be helpful but they will. There will be a live mindfulness session after the HOPE course finishes and it is a really great session if you can join it. The link is in the news feed.  Amanda 😊

@Mikki Hi Mikki, This is a great description, a word cloud in your head. You are absolutely not failing at it by having words and thoughts pop into you head this is absolutely what happens. Recognising and acknowledging the thoughts as they come in can be helpful but they will. There will be a live mindfulness session after the HOPE course finishes and it is a really great session if you can join it. The link is in the news feed.  Amanda 😊
I noticed that one same thought kept appearing during this exercise, but overall it was a very calming exercise.

@sinead4 It's great that you noticed the thought, it's the noticing and letting it go that is important. 😊

I'm sure many others have experienced the same thought coming up during sessions like the thought leaves I know I certainly have. Each time we practice we will have different experiences.

I really enjoyed this exercise, I felt myself relaxing into my chair and I had lots of thoughts flowing in such a short period of time. I like to brain-dump all my thoughts onto a piece of paper, so this is a great exercise to do after. I have been told that I am quite the overthinker so I have saved this to my Youtube to revisit.
@olivia01 Really glad that you found this exercise useful. I really like it too. Great to save it to revisit Amanda 😊
I think this could be a useful tool.
@toni8 Great feedback the thought leaves activity is one of my favourite activities too.  I am glad you have found it useful. Amanda ☺
This was a very good activity I will be doing it again 👌
This was a very good activity I will be doing it again 👌
This was a very good activity I will be doing it again 👌
@jody3 I am so pleased you found it helpful. 🙂
@jody3  I love the thought leaves activity glad you found it useful ☺
I found this tricky, think it's going to be one I'll have to practice. I can definitely see how it can be useful!

@bethanie8 This is great reflection and not every activity will be right for everyone. I like to think of the HOPE activities as a tool box where you can draw on them as tools at different times and situations. Amanda ☺
Managing stress in one of my greatest challenges. I particularly enjoyed the streams exercise and will research more about mindfulness.

@lynn24 Managing stress is really challenging as you say but recognising that you are experiencing stress is   a big step in the right direction. Glad you enjoyed the thought leaves exercise. There is a link in the news forum to the IIH UK video which is a quick mindfulness exercise and a great place to start. Amanda ☺
Thank you, will give it a go!
I really like this activity, I have used it before and it has been so helpful during anxious times.
@olivia24 I am pleased you found this activity helpful and it is now a tool you can use anytime you need to. 🙂
I really enjoyed this activity and found it very calming. It reminded me of my trip to Cornwall last year and bathing my feet in the River Valency in Boscastle. A very happy memory.
This is a great activity, you can really lose yourself by your stream of leaves, and I found it very useful. I will definitely do this at least once a day
I found this very relaxing and carming to the point it made me sleepy.Im going to practice this every other night ..

@kristy2 It’s lovely how the activity helped you tap back into that feeling — almost like giving your nervous system a reminder of calm. Cornwall and moments like that can stay with us in such a grounding way. I’m so glad it brought something happy back for you 💛

@laura174 I love how you described that — “losing yourself in your stream of leaves” sounds so immersive 🍃 It’s great that you found it genuinely useful, and building it in once a day sounds like a lovely way to create a small pocket of calm. I’m so glad it resonated with you
@zara4 that sounds wonderful 💛 It’s great that it was so relaxing it even made you sleepy! Practising every other night sounds like a lovely, gentle routine to help you unwind and recharge 😌

After being diagnosed with IIH  in April and then having a heart attack in June and losing my mother in law Aug
I enjoy each day as if it could be my last.

Hi Sheila, I am sorry it sounds like you have had a very challenging year. Enjoying the moment is so important as you say and sometimes sadly it is big life events that remind us of this. It sounds like the HOPE programme is happening at an important time for you and I hope it helps you to find some support strategies. Amanda 😊
@sheila15 I am sorry to hear that you have had such a challenging year. I hope that the HOPE course will support you and help you with strategies. Amanda 😊
I focus on my health more. Less hobbies more exercise. In fact exercise has now become a hobby.
@lizzie11 Hi Lizzie, Exercise is a great hobby. I find exercise really helps me too. Is there any particular exercise you find helpful? Amanda

Mum guilt not engaging enough with my daughter. Blocking partner out trying to handle things on my own as don’t feel he understands. But the video makes me need to realise and prioritise family the rocks.

@amy79 Juggling children, partners life with a long term condition is really hard and your feelings are totally valid. Know that you aren't alone and there are people out there who get it. We have another HOPE cafe coming up if you plus or minus partner are able to attend they can be really useful. Do reach out though if Kerry and I can help support you in any way. Amanda ☺

My dad used this story in his speech at my wedding but I sort of forgot. I get the mum guilt thing. I am trying to learn to pace myself and let them learn to do stuff too. Reforming my control freak tendencies is taking time mind you!
Emma, I am with you and your reflection is great here. Mum guilt is such a challenge. Its so hard letting them learn to do things and not step in... I struggle with this too. Amanda ☺
@emma223 What a lovely memory that your dad used this story at your wedding. Mum guilt is hard and not stepping in even harder. I think every awesome mum a bit of this too. Amanda ☺

My diagnosis of IIH is relatively new (April 2025), I feel like I am still at the stage where I am struggling to come to terms with how my life plan has had to change. This video is really thought provoking, because even if I can't go to work currently or do the things I used to do, I have rocks in my life who I should focus on and appreciate more.

For me IIH has changed my life significantly. Work was always something I enjoyed and was important to me. Unfortunately IIH made it impossible for me to continue to work. I am now medically retired and the future looks very different for me. This video is a reminder of what the most important parts of my life are and to focus on those blessings.

@hannah70  Hannah I am glad you found the lifes priorities video thought provoking and that focusing on the rocks is your life is important. You might want to draw on these in a gratitude post? Amanda ☺

@ruth40 Ruth what you say resonates strongly with me as I had to medically retire from work too which is one of the hardest things I have ever done. Thank you for sharing. It is really positive to hear that the life's priorities video was useful for you and absolutely to focus on the blessings of the important things in our lives. These can make lovely gratitudes. Amanda ☺
To see as many of my friends as possible, while I still have some sight.
@sheila19 Sorry to hear that you have visual challenges. Spending times with friends is very important. Amanda ☺
I think health in general has become much more of a priority for me since my diagnosis. I feel like I need to do the little things now to look after myself a bit more.

@bethanie8 This is such a valid point. Prioritising your health and the little things that can support you definitely come to the fore when you are diagnosed with a condition like IIH. I hope that the materials on the HOPE course support you with ways to do this too.  Amanda ☺
Work was a big part of my life, but had to accept medical retirement. I now have a new job, and trying hard not yo let it take over my life, and make more time for family and enjoying life
